info.title: Using systemd-coredump to debug application crashes

abstract.para: You can use systemd-coredump to debug application crashes.

topic.para: systemd-coredump collects and displays core dumps to analyze application crashes. A core dump is an image of the process memory at the time of termination.
By default, when a process crashes, systemd-coredump performs the following actions:

listitem.para: Logs the event to the systemd journal.

topic.para: You can examine the dump file using coredumpctl or external tools such as gdb or crash. For more information on analysis tools, see [Section 20.8, Analyzing the Crash Dump].

note.para: Core dumps stored in /var/lib/systemd/coredump/ are automatically deleted after three days. This is configured through the d /var/lib/systemd/coredump line in /usr/lib/tmpfiles.d/systemd.conf.

section.title: Use and Configuration
section.para: systemd-coredump is enabled by default. The primary configuration file is located at /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.
The following parameters in [Coredump] define how dumps are handled:

listitem.para: Storage: Defines where to store dumps such as none, external, journal. By default the dumps are saved in external.
Compress: Enables compression for the stored core dump. By default, this is enabled.
ProcessSizeMax: The maximum size of a core dump to be processed. The default size is 2G
ExternalSizeMax: The maximum size for a dump stored on disk. The default size is 2G

section.para: Size units are B, K, M, G, T, P, and E. ExternalSizeMax also supports a value of infinity.

note.para: A new instance of systemd-coredump is invoked for every crash. Configuration changes are applied immediately to the next crash; you do not need to restart any services.
